coredata.dc:title: A novel molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor modified with carbon dots, chitosan, gold nanoparticles for the determination of patulin
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Wei
Pi, Fuwei
Zhang, Hongxia
Sun, Jiadi
Zhang, Yinzhi
Sun, Xiulan
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, molecular imprinting technique was applied to the electrochemical sensor. We used 2-oxindole as dummy template, ρ-Aminothiophenol (ρ-ATP) as functional monomers, combined with the high sensitivity of electrochemical detection, to achieve a specific and efficient detection of patulin in fruit juice. In addition, carbon dots and chitosan were used as the modifying material to improve electron-transfer rate, expand the electroactive surface of glassy carbon electrode and enhance strength of the signal. The Au–S bond and hydrogen bond were employed to complete the assembly of the ρ-ATP and 2-oxindole on the surface of the electrode. Then, polymer membranes were formed by electropolymerization in a polymer solution containing ρ-ATP, HAuCl4, tetrabutylammonium perchlorate (TBAP) and the template molecule 2- oxindole. After elution, the specific cavity can adsorb the target patulin. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) measurements were performed to monitor the electropolymerization process and its optimization. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and Atomic force microscopy (AFM) analyses were used for characterization. This was the first time that the molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) technology combined with carbon dots, chitosan and Au nanoparticles modification and was applied in the electrochemical detection of patulin. The linear response range of the MIP sensor was from 1 × 10–12 to 1 × 10−9 molL−1 and the limit of detection (LOD) was 7.57 × 10–13 molL−1. The sensor had a high-speed real-time detection capability, low sample consumption, high sensitivity, low interference, good stability and could become a new promising method for the detection of patulin.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Patulin
Molecularly imprinted polymer
Carbon dots
Chitosan
Electrochemical sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The preparation of molecularly imprinted membrane and condition optimization
Optimization of the conditions for the patulin detection based on molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor
the optimization of elution time for template
the optimization of adsorption time for target
Selectivity, reproducibility and stability of the MIP sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of the CDs. (B) UV–vis absorption spectra (Abs) and fluorescence spectra of the CDs intensity.
(A) AFM image of the bare GCE (B) AFM image of the CS-CDs/GCE. (C) SEM image of the bare GCE. (D) SEM image of the CS-CDs/GCE.

(A) Summary chart of the CV measurements with five different electrodes in 2.5mmolL−1 [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution containing 0.1molL−1 KCl. (a) bare GCE (b) Au/GCE (c) Au/CS/GCE (d) Au/CS-CDs/GCE. (B) CVs for Au/CS-CDs/GCE in 2.5mmolL−1 [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution containing 0.1molL−1 KCl (scan rate: 80–150mVs-1, scan range: − 0.2 to 0.6V). (C) The relationships of the Ipa and Ipc with v 1/2 for Au/CS-CDs/GCE. (D) The CV measurements for bare GCE in 2.5mmolL−1 [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution containing 0.1molL−1 KCl (scan rate: 80–150mVs−1, scan range: − 0.2 to 0.6V). (E) The relationships of the Ipa and Ipc with v 1/2 for bare GCE.

(A) CV measurements and (B) DPV measurements for (a) Au/CS-CDs/GCE, (b) MIP-Au/CS-CDs/GCE, (c) NIP-Au/CS-CDs/GCE after the elution process, (d) MIP-Au/CS-CDs/GCE after template removal, (e) MIP-Au/CS-CDs/GCE after rebinding in 2.5 × 10−10 molL−1 patulin solution.

(A) DPV measurements of MIP-Au/CS-CDs/GCE in 2.5mmolL−1 [Fe(CN)6] 3−/4− containing 0.1molL−1 KCl preconcentrated using different concentrations of patulin: (a–k) 1 × 10−13, 5 × 10−13, 1 × 10−12, 5 × 10−12, 1 × 10−11, 5 × 10–11, 1 × 10−10, 5 × 10−10, 1 × 10−9, 5 × 10−9 and 1 × 10−8 mol L−1 patulin in an aqueous solution for 200s (B) DPVs of MIP-Au/CS-CDS/GCE and MIP-Au/GCE in 2.5mmolL−1 [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− containing 0.1molL−1 KCl preconcentrated using different concentrations of patulin (from 1 × 10–12 to 1 × 10−9 mol L−1). (C) Linear relationship between the peak current and logarithm concentration of patulin for the MIP-Au/CS-CDs/GCE and MIP-Au/GCE.
Current response changes corresponding to the MIP-Au/CS-CDs/GCE for different concentrations of patulin, 5-hydroxymethyl-furfural, alternariol and alternariol methyl ether.

head.ce:title: A novel molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor modified with carbon dots, chitosan, gold nanoparticles for the determination of patulin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food Science and Technology, National Engineering Research Center for Functional Food, Synergetic Innovation Center of Food Safety and quality Control, Jiangnan University, Wuxi, Jiangsu 214122, P.R. China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Technology, National Engineering Research Center for Functional Food, Synergetic Innovation Center of Food Safety and quality Control, Jiangnan University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Foreign Studies, Shaanxi University of Technology, 723000, P.R. China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Foreign Studies, Shaanxi University of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, molecular imprinting technique was applied to the electrochemical sensor. We used 2-oxindole as dummy template, ρ-Aminothiophenol (ρ-ATP) as functional monomers, combined with the high sensitivity of electrochemical detection, to achieve a specific and efficient detection of patulin in fruit juice. In addition, carbon dots and chitosan were used as the modifying material to improve electron-transfer rate, expand the electroactive surface of glassy carbon electrode and enhance strength of the signal. The Au–S bond and hydrogen bond were employed to complete the assembly of the ρ-ATP and 2-oxindole on the surface of the electrode. Then, polymer membranes were formed by electropolymerization in a polymer solution containing ρ-ATP, HAuCl4, tetrabutylammonium perchlorate (TBAP) and the template molecule 2- oxindole. After elution, the specific cavity can adsorb the target patulin. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) measurements were performed to monitor the electropolymerization process and its optimization. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and Atomic force microscopy (AFM) analyses were used for characterization. This was the first time that the molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) technology combined with carbon dots, chitosan and Au nanoparticles modification and was applied in the electrochemical detection of patulin. The linear response range of the MIP sensor was from 1 × 10–12 to 1 × 10−9 molL−1 and the limit of detection (LOD) was 7.57 × 10–13 molL−1. The sensor had a high-speed real-time detection capability, low sample consumption, high sensitivity, low interference, good stability and could become a new promising method for the detection of patulin.

ce:section.ce:para: Patulin (4-hydroxy-4H-furo[3,2-c]pyran-2(6H)-one), a type of polyketide lactone, is a toxic secondary metabolite produced by numerous species of filamentous fungi belonging to the genera Penicillium, Aspergillus and Byssochlamys (Magan and Olsen, 2004; Puel et al., 2010). Patulin was first discovered as an antibiotic, but was found to be toxic to animals soon after. Currently, there are a series of toxicity symptoms which can indicate the acute, sub-acute and chronic health risks of patulin such as agitation, convulsions, dyspnea, pulmonary congestion, ulceration, edema, hyperemia, intestinal inflammation and epithelial cell degeneration (Moake et al., 2005; Song et al., 2014). Given the serious effect of patulin on body health, the levels of patulin in foods are regulated in many countries worldwide. In most cases, the maximum value has been set at 50μg/L (3.25 × 10−7 molL−1) in juices and 25μg/kg (1.62 × 10−7 molkg−1) in solid apple (Pires et al., 2012; Puel et al., 2010). Some countries, such as those of the European Union, imposed even more stringent standards which regulate the maximum patulin concentration in food products intended for infants and young children at 10μg/kg (6.5 × 10−8 molkg−1) (Puel et al., 2010).

Although the confirmatory methods such as chromatographies (Cunha et al., 2009; Katerere et al., 2007; Rychlik and Schieberle, 1999; Scott et al., 1972; Tsao and Zhou, 2000) and immunoassays (Funari et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2008; Pennacchio et al., 2014 , 2015) have already been proved to possess excellent reproducibility and satisfactory sensitivity for both qualitative and quantitative analysis, unfortunately and inevitably, the defects of these methods are also obvious. In particular, antibodies used in the immunoassay are very expensive, difficult to find and less reproducible. In addition, patulin and artificial antigen which enter the animal body will quickly be degraded (Fliege and Metzler, 2000), so the development of a commercial kit for patulin detection remains stagnant. To solve these pressing problems, molecular imprinting technique are proposed in this study.

Molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP), a three-dimensional polymeric matrix, has outstanding specific adsorption properties for the substance which is expected to detect under the complex matrix interference (Blanco-López et al., 2004). several research have demonstrated that MIPs are good alternatives to antibody-based sorbents and have replaced immunoassays to become a new rapid detection method (Lavignac et al., 2004). Indeed, it can be predicted that the MIP technique will become one of the promising methods in the sensor field for its low cost, simplicity, reliability and wide choice of templates and functional monomers.

Furthermore, MIP electrochemical sensors have attracted considerable attention and have been widely utilized to detect different molecules ranging from small hazardous molecules to biomacromolecules (Kan et al., 2012; Li et al., 2012). Compared with traditional detection methods, the electrochemical sensor is more sensitive, more convenient, has faster processing speed, and lower cost (Apetrei and Apetrei, 2015). Combined with the MIP technique, it can effectively overcome the interference of impurity, which is the main drawback of electrochemical detection. In order to obtain ideal MIP electrochemical sensors, it is necessary to modify the electrode to get better electrical activity and pay more attention to the fabrication of the MIP on the electrode surfaces.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a class of ‘zero-dimensional’ carbon Nanomaterials (Zhu et al., 2009). Compared with other electrode-modified materials, the CDs are bound to more and more important and gain more and more attention due to their low cytotoxicity, excellent biocompatibility, simple synthesis, low cost and remarkable conductivity (Liu et al., 2012). However, the exploration of CDs as a type of modified material for electrochemical sensors to monitor analytes with high selectivity and sensitivity still remains at an early stage (Dai et al., 2012). Au nanoparticles (AuNPs) have been intensively studied for the fabrication of electrodes. Due to the large specific surface area of AuNPs, their conductivity is one of the most significant properties which can be used for amplifying electric signal (Liu and Wulff, 2004). Chitosan (CS) is a linear polysaccharide composed of randomly distributed b-(1e4)-linked D-glucosamine (deacetylated unit) and N-acetyl-D-glucosamine (acetylated unit)(Rinaudo, 2006). Because of its excellent film-forming ability, high permeability, good adhesion, nontoxicity, low cost and susceptibility to chemical modification, CS can be used as a dispersant to form a stable film on the electrode surface with various modified materials.

In this study, a novel, rapid convenient and highly sensitive electrochemical sensor for the determination of patulin based on MIP and electropolymerization technology has been constructed. In order to improve the electrochemical performance, we triple -modified the electrode with CDs, CS, and AuNPs to increase its electroactive areas and acquire distinct electric signal. A molecularly imprinted film was fabricated on the modified electrode surface adopting the method of surface molecular self-assembly and co-polymerization of functional monomer and template molecule. Instead of the target molecule itself, we adopted a dummy template 2-oxindole to replace patulin with the purpose of forming the molecularly imprinted cavity easily at a lower cost. To our knowledge, this is the first time that CDs, CS and AuNPs have been combined to modify an electrode with MIP by using the dummy template 2-oxindole and used to detect the presence of patulin in apple juice. The sensor had rapid equilibrium adsorption time, high binding affinity and selectivity towards patulin.
All reagents and equipment required for the experiment, and detailed experimental steps were included in the Supplementary materials.

The whole preparation procedures of the MIP-Au/CS-CDs/GCE was briefly illustrated in Scheme 1A. The preparation process can be divided into the following steps: (1) Modification of the electrode with CS-CDs. (2) Electrodeposition of AuNPs on the surface of the CS-CDs/GCE. (3) Self-assembly of ρ-ATP monomer onto the Au/ CS-CDs/GCE. (4) Hydrogen bonding adsorption of 2-oxindole template on the surface of ρ-ATP. (5) Co-polymerization of the functional monomer ρ-ATP and template 2-oxindole to fabricate the MIP on the surface of the Au/CS-CDs/GCE. (6) Removal of the template 2-oxindole from the molecular imprinted membranes.

The morphology of the CDs was characterized by TEM ( Fig. 1A). The image of CDs demonstrated well dispersed and spherical nanoparticles in narrow distributions of 7 ± 1nm. The typical UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the CDs were shown in Fig. 1B. The spectral data clearly showed that a strong UV absorption band appeared at 268nm, and when the CDs were excited at 344nm, the PL spectra showed a broad and symmetrical emission peak at 463nm.

To further enhance the sensitivity of the electrode, we modified the electrode with CS-CDs and AuNPs. The bare GCE surface was relatively smooth with an average roughness (Rq) of 2.15nm, as shown in the AFM images ( Fig. 2A). In comparison, the average roughness (Rq) of the GCE was 9.32nm after being modified by the CS-CDs and AuNPs (Fig. 2B), so that we can clearly observe the distinct rough bumps. The result meant that the CS-CDs and AuNPs film was deposited on the GCE surface, furthermore the conductivity of the electrode can be increased due to the larger electric surface area. The SEM images also confirmed, as seen in Fig. 2C, that the surface of the bare GCE exhibited a relatively flat structure with little roughness. The data presented in Fig. 2D showed that the CS-CDs and AuNPs distributed almost homogeneously on the surface of the GCE, exhibiting more roughness and wrinkles, which improved the electroactive surface of the bare GCE.

The electrochemical properties of the triple-modified Au/CS-CDs/GCE were investigated using 2.5mmolL−1 [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− as the electrochemical probe. The CV measurements of the bare GCE, Au/GCE, Au/CS/GCE and Au/CS-CDs/GCE in the presence of 2.5mmolL−1 [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− were presented in Fig. 3A. Evidently, we can see that the electrical conductivity of the bare GCE electrode was inferior than that of the bare GCE after gold plating (Au/GCE), which gave proof to the conductivity enhancement of the AuNPs. Moreover, in order to successfully connect the functional monomer (ρ-ATP), it was a necessary and essential step to introduce AuNPs on the surface of the electrode for the establishment of the Au-S bond. That was the reason why we chose a GCE with gold plating rather than a bare GCE to conduct the next experiment of MIP electropolymerization. Comparing the current intensity of Au/GCE, Au/CS/GCE and Au/CS-CDs/GCE, we could determine that our modification had a very good effect, and further demonstrate that not only CS but also the CDs could really improve the electrochemical behavior. Overall, we chosen the GCE as basement, modified the CS-CDs on its surface and then electroplated AuNPs to complete the whole final modification of the electrode. The CV curves for Au/CS-CDs/GCE and bare GCE, at scan rates from 80 to150mVs−1, were shown in Figs. 3B and D. Both the anodic and cathodic peak currents, Ipa and Ipc, respectively, were linearly related to the square scan rate, according to the linear regression equations Ipa (μA) = 8.5551v 1/2 + 91.492 (R2 = 0.9943), Ipc (μA) = −6.2384 v1/2 − 84.205 (R2 = 0.9953) for Au/CS-CDs/GCE (Fig. 3C) and Ipa (μA) = 2.7143v 1/2 + 11.644 (R2 = 0.9941), Ipc (μA) = −2.0261v 1/2 − 12.682 (R2 = 0.9901) for bare GCE (Fig. 3E). The results indicated that the process was predominantly diffusion-controlled (Laviron, 1979), which suggested that Au/CS-CDs played a role in increasing the electron-transfer rate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The preparation of molecularly imprinted membrane and condition optimization
ce:section.ce:para: The one-step electropolymerization of MIP, covered on the surface of the Au/ CS-CDs/GCE, was performed in a solution containing 60mmolL−1 ρ-ATP, 0.2g/L HAuCl4, 10mmolL−1 2-oxindole, 50mmolL−1 TBAP by 10 CV scannings in the potential window between − 0.3 and + 1.2. The electrochemical process used to form a MIP film on Au/CS-CDs/GCE was depicted in Fig. S1 (A).

An oxidation-reduction process appeared during the first cycle (denoted by the red line) and disappeared during the second cycle. The reduction peak and oxidation peak were clearly observed at potentials of approximately 0.58V and 0.29V. It can be clarified that the Au3+ in HAuCl4 was reduced to Au1+ or Au0 and absorbed onto the electrode surface and then the polymerization reaction between ρ-ATP and AuNPs occurred. With the increase of consecutive scan cycles, an insulating polymer layer was created, which restrained electron transport between the surface of the electrode and the electrolyte, thus the current decreased considerably and eventually reached a steady state with almost no response. Similarly, the NIP was also performed under the same conditions without adding 2-oxindole to the polymerization solution.

To optimize the synthesis conditions of MIP, a series of MIP-Au/CS-CDs/GCE sensors were prepared at different ratio of template to monomer (1:2, 1:4, 1:6, 1:8) to evaluate the most suitable ratio. The ratio of template to monomer in the electropolymerization solution was closely related to the quantity of imprinting recognition sites in MIP. In general, increasing the dosage of the monomer would lead to the full assembly of the molecular imprinting polymer, but redundant monomer would cause self-polymerization and reduce the selective binding sites instead. After template removal, the MIP with the 1:6 ratios of template to monomer had the best current response, as shown in Fig. S1(B), which meant MIP generated many holes that provided the specific binding sites and conducted electricity. Accordingly, in this study, we adopted the 1:6 ratio of the template to monomer to synthesize the MIP.

The relationship between the peak current and surface conditions of the modified electrode was depicted in Fig. 4A. As we anticipated, deposited with the CS-CDs and AuNPs, the electrode had a good electric behavior (as shown by curve a). After the formation of MIP, almost no current response was in the electrochemical system (as shown by curve b), due to blocking of the transfer of electron by the compact membrane. However, when the template was removed, the conductive path between the electrolyte and electrode formed again, so the reduction and oxidation peaks increased from curve b to curve d. Then if the target molecule appeared, they would be adsorbed into the cavity, which caused the reduction of the electrical conductivity (as shown from curve d to curve e). In contrast, if the NIP (without the template molecule adding) was formed, there would be no conductive voids after elution and almost no current response can be seen (as shown by curve c). The corresponding graph of the DPV measurements was shown in Fig. 4B. The contact angle experiment and the SEM examination of MIP-Au/CS-CDs/GCE and NIP-Au/CS-CDs/GCE were shown in Fig. S2 and S3. The contact angle θ was 64.7°, which demonstrated that the MIP film was hydrophilic (Fig. S2). The rough and porous structures seen in the SEM images (Fig. S3(A)) indicated that the recognition sites had been generated in the dummy-template MIP, which can be attributed to the removal of the template. As a comparison, there were almost no recognition sites in the NIP (Fig. S3(B)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the conditions for the patulin detection based on molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor
the optimization of elution time for template
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain a satisfactory sensitive, selective and reproducible electrochemical sensor, it was very important to remove the template from the MIP-Au/CS-CDs/GCE and remove the target patulin for repeated use. Considering that the hydrogen bonding was the main binding force between the functional monomer and the template/target molecule, we first decided to use acetic acid, hydrochloric acid, sulfuric acid, and nitric acid as eluents. Among these acids, patulin could react with nitrate and generate oxalate, maintain high stability, not easily be eluted in hydrochloric acid, and could be hydrolyzed in the sulfuric acid or sodium hydroxide environment. Thus we chose the sulfuric acid as eluent, replaced the template and target molecules and finally completed the elution process.

We performed optimization of the elution time, which as shown in the Fig. S4 (A) and (B), when the electrode was immersed in sulfuric acid for 12min, the current reached the maximum value and then along with the increase of the elution time, current intensity almost underwent no change, accordingly, we applied 12min as the best elution time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: the optimization of adsorption time for target
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption time was also an important parameter for the improvement of the assay efficiency, which determined whether the target was sufficiently adsorbed onto the electrode surface. The amount of patulin adsorbed onto the MIP sensor increased with increasing adsorption time as shown in Fig. S4(C), and the kinetics of adsorption (shown in Fig. S4(D)) indicated that patulin was quickly absorbed onto the MIP sensor and the adsorption reached kinetic equilibrium within 200s.

For patulin detection, the prepared MIP-Au/CS-CDs/GCE was immersed in different concentrations of patulin solutions (from 1 × 10−13 to 1 × 10−8 molL−1). In solution, only patulin can be adsorbed in the molecular imprinting cavity, which hindered electron transfer from the [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- ion pair to the electrode surface, causing the decline of the current peak. The extent of the decline was related to the concentration of patulin, as shown in Fig. 5(A). With the increase of the concentration of patulin concentration, more cavities would be filled up, and that would give rise to a more significant decrease in the peak current. In order to illustrate the influence of the CS-CDs, we used the Au/GCE as the basement, electroplating MIP to compare with the performance of MIP-Au/CS-CDs/GCE. The results (Fig. 5(B)) demonstrated that compared with the MIP-Au/CS-CDs/GCE, the extent of current fluctuation of the MIP-Au/GCE influenced by the concentration of patulin was much weaker. In other words, adopting the approach of CS-CDs modification, the MIP sensor had a large-scale, sensitive, and credible detection range (Fig. 5(C)). The limit of detection (LOD) was much better than that of the MIP sensor lacking the CS-CDs (2.38 × 10−12 molL−1). a linear relationship was observed between the peak current and the logarithm concentration of patulin covering the range from 1 × 10−12 to 1 × 10−9 mol L−1, the linear regression equation I(μA) = − 21.111logC (molL−1) – 125.74 (R2 = 0.9907). The detection limit was found to be 7.57 × 10−13 molL−1 based on the 3σ of the blank signals, which was more sensitive than most available patulin detection methods reported to date (Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, reproducibility and stability of the MIP sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity was an important index to evaluate the quality of the sensor. After elution of the template, because no target was adsorbed on the surface of the sensor, the peak value of the current reached a maximum. (represented by I0). We placed a MIP sensor in the detection solution, and recorded the current in time (represented by I). Having add and changed the concentration of the target, we then observed the relationship between ΔI(I0-I) and the target concentration. Here we chose 5-hydroxymethyl-furfural, alternariol and alternariol methyl ether as the controls to assess the specificity of this method based on the MIP-Au/CS-CDs/GCE. The molecular structures of 5-hydroxymethyl-furfural, alternariol, alternariol methyl ether, which were usually present in apple juice and the structural analogue to patulin are shown in Fig. S5. Due to the specificity of the sensor for the adsorption of its target-patulin, only the sensor immersed in the patulin solution experienced a drastic decline of electrical conductivity (a significant increase of ΔI/I0), while the ΔI/I0 of other sensors, which were not immersed in the patulin solution, exhibited almost no change, as indicated by the results shown in Fig. 6. We evaluated the reproducibility of the MIP-Au/CS-CDs/GCE, by placing the modified electrode into patulin solution at a concentration of 2.5 × 10−10 molL−1 and observing the change of DPV in the graph plot. In this experiment, 3 electrodes were selected for detection (each electrode was applied 3 times). The results revealed a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 3.32%, thus, the MIP-Au/CS-CDs/GCE exhibited excellent reproducibility. In addition, we also evaluated the stability of the sensor. By storing the electrode under the conditions of clean and dry air with the protection of cover at room temperature. Afterwards, the lifetime of the MIP-Au/CS-CDs/GCE was investigated by measuring the DPV response in the 2.5mmolL−1 [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution containing 0.1molL−1 KCl every other week. The results indicated that the response of the imprinted electrode decreased to 90% after being stored for 28days and 80% of the original response was retained after 63 days.

We added 0.005, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5 and 1nmolL−1 patulin solution to fresh apple juice obtained from the supermarket, and then measured the patulin content of the samples in triplicate (n = 3). The measurement data revealed a recovery rate ranging from 96% to 98.7% with a RSD varying from 7.3% to 10.4% (Table S2). The results demonstrated that the new method described in this study can be used for the accurate detection of patulin in actual sample such as apple juice.

In this work, molecularly imprinted technique was applied to electrochemical detection for patulin, which could enhance the selectivity to target materials. Furthermore, after triple modification of CS, CDs and AuNPs, the sensitivity of the sensor was improved ( linear range: 1 × 10−12–1 × 10−9 molL−1, limit of detection (LOD): 7.57 × 10−13 molL−1). The whole process was simple and controllable and it was promising for practical application as a novel method for the detection of patulin.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work has been supported by the National Research Program of China (Nos. 2016YFD0401204, 201513006 and JUSRP51501), Collaborative Innovation Center for Food Safety and Quality Control and the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2017.06.036.
